finding: Canonicalization of an already-parsed JavaScript object cannot prove that the source JSON text had unique object member names because common parsers retain only one value. JCS requires I-JSON input and explicitly forbids duplicate property names, so uniqueness must be checked before ordinary JSON parsing collapses the evidence.
design_implication: Text-origin evidence must pass a strict duplicate-member scanner before JSON.parse and RFC 8785 canonicalization. Escaped and literal spellings of the same decoded name are duplicates, while identical names in different nested objects remain valid.

verification.environment: isolated Node runtime with byte-identical artifact and existing canonical-json-digest dependency
covered: stable digest across whitespace and property order

evidence_strength: committed deterministic prototype plus primary-specification requirement
uncertainty: The scanner is purpose-built and has not yet been differentially fuzzed against an independent streaming JSON parser or the JSONTestSuite corpus.
falsification_route: Generate grammar-based JSON texts, compare acceptance with an independent duplicate-preserving parser, and require every accepted text to round-trip through JSON.parse and the existing RFC 8785 conformance vectors. Any missed decoded-name collision or valid-text rejection falsifies the gate.
trust_limit: Passing this gate proves only syntactic uniqueness and compatibility with the downstream canonicalizer; it does not prove source authenticity, semantic truth, or builder trust.
